articles.title: Ken Russell’s Uncensored Version Of ‘The Devils’ Receives First Public Screening At Cannes 55 Years After It Was Produced
articles.why: Ken Russell's 'The Devils' — one of the most suppressed films in cinema history — receiving its first-ever public uncensored screening at Cannes, 55 years after it was made, is a genuine archival milestone and a story about what gets to exist.
articles.hook: The most banned film in British history / just screened publicly for the first time. / 55 years later.

articles.title: Elon Musk’s SpaceXAI has been bleeding staff since its merger
articles.why: More than 50 employees quietly bleeding out of SpaceXAI since its merger is a concrete signal that the forced consolidation is failing on a human level — burnout, leadership chaos, and liquidity-event retention collapse are the real story behind Musk's empire-building.
articles.hook: 50 people gone since the merger. / Nobody announced it.

articles.title: Leaked images reveal Xbox Elite 3 controller with mysterious new buttons
articles.why: The Xbox Elite 3 leaking through a government regulator — complete with mysterious new buttons — is the kind of accidental reveal that shapes the gaming conversation for weeks. The Elite line is the gold standard for serious console players.
articles.hook: Brazil's regulator just leaked the Xbox Elite 3. / New buttons nobody can explain yet.

articles.title: Adidas and Song for the Mute Are Dropping Their Freshest Collection Yet
articles.why: Adidas x Song for the Mute is a genuinely sharp menswear collaboration with real cultural credibility — the 007 tie-in adds a pop culture layer that extends its reach beyond core fashion audiences.
articles.hook: Adidas found its most interesting collab partner yet / Not who you'd expect.

articles.title: How to Watch ‘Off Campus,’ the Steamy College Soap That’s Being Compared to ‘Heated Rivalry’
articles.why: 'Off Campus' arriving as the steamy college sports romance being compared to 'Heated Rivalry' signals the genre has real cultural momentum — romantasy and sports romance are the dominant consumer fiction categories right now.
articles.hook: The college hockey romance everyone's comparing to 'Heated Rivalry' / just dropped on Prime.

articles.title: Amazon Studios Accused of Blacklisting Vendor Who Refused to Pay Kickbacks
articles.why: A post-production vendor suing Amazon Studios for allegedly blacklisting him after refusing to pay kickbacks is a concrete, named allegation of corruption inside one of Hollywood's most powerful studios — and it's the kind of story that rarely surfaces.
articles.hook: He wouldn't pay the kickback. / Amazon never called again.

articles.title: YouTube viewers watch 2 billion hours of Shorts on TVs each month
articles.why: Two billion hours of YouTube Shorts watched on TVs every month inverts the entire premise of short-form video — built for phones, it's migrating to the big screen, which has real implications for how advertisers and creators think about the format.
articles.hook: Shorts were built for your phone. / Two billion hours a month, people watch them on TV.

articles.title: Gen Z Is Pioneering a New Understanding of Truth
articles.why: The argument that Gen Z has developed a fundamentally different relationship to truth — not relativism, but a fluency in blended fact and feeling born from growing up inside algorithmic feeds — is one of the more useful frameworks for understanding generational communication breakdowns.
articles.hook: Gen Z didn't lose track of truth. / They built a different one.

articles.title: Pay Attention
articles.why: Jonathan Haidt delivering his full NYU commencement address — on attention, truth, and the cost of a phone-saturated adolescence — at a moment when his work on teen mental health is more culturally central than ever, makes this worth reading in full.
articles.hook: Haidt gave a commencement speech. / Students objected to him being there. / He gave it anyway.

articles.title: A Very Pretentious Form of Propaganda
articles.why: The Venice Biennale is contemporary art's highest-stakes stage, and this year's American pavilion — arriving amid the Iran war and domestic political fracture — is generating real critical discomfort. A useful cultural temperature check.
articles.hook: America sent an art show to Venice. / Critics left feeling mocked for paying attention.

articles.title: Americans really don't want AI data centers close to their homes
articles.why: Survey data showing Americans don't want AI data centers near their homes — amid growing stories of noise, water use, and energy displacement — signals that the infrastructure backlash is becoming a political force, not just an environmental concern.
articles.hook: Americans are turning NIMBY on AI data centers. / Noise. Water. Power grid. It's personal now.

articles.title: Men use "vocal fry" more than women, counter to stereotype
articles.why: Research overturning the assumption that vocal fry is a female speech habit — finding men actually use it more — is a clean, counterintuitive science story that challenges a deeply embedded cultural stereotype about how women talk.
articles.hook: Vocal fry was supposed to be a women thing. / Men do it more. / The stereotype was always backwards.

articles.title: Cell phone users can't stop incriminating themselves
articles.why: A compendium of cases where search history and phone data became the deciding evidence in criminal trials — including a murder conviction — is a vivid reminder of how completely smartphones have eliminated the possibility of private thought before a crime.
articles.hook: She Googled 'what kind of doctor was Dr Pepper.' / Her other searches got her convicted of murder.

articles.title: Judge probes whether Musk settlement with Trump admin is tainted by corruption
articles.why: A federal judge refusing to rubber-stamp the Musk-SEC settlement — and openly questioning whether it reflects corrupt favoritism — is a rare moment of judicial pushback that could expose the full scope of the administration's relationship with Musk.
articles.hook: A judge won't sign Musk's SEC deal. / Says it smells like corruption. / She wants to look closer.
